mnemonics.title: BODHA Order
mnemonics.content: Binary, Octal, Decimal, Hexa — All base numbering systems in order of increasing base (2, 8, 10, 16). Think: 'BODHA' sounds like 'Buddha' — a wise system!

mnemonics.title: 2's Complement Song
mnemonics.content: To find 2's complement: Flip all bits (1→0, 0→1), then add 1. Remember: 'Flip and Fix' — first flip, then add to fix the sign!

mnemonics.title: Hex Fingers
mnemonics.content: 0-9 for values 0-9, A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15. Remember: A=10, B=11... F=15 — Like the alphabet starts at age 10!

mnemonics.title: Binary Place Values
mnemonics.content: 128 64 32 16 8 4 2 1 — From right to left, double each time. 'Royal Kings Have Cups Of Fine Beer' (128,64,32,16,8,4,2,1)

mnemonics.title: Octal & Hex Conversion
mnemonics.content: 3 binary digits = 1 octal digit; 4 binary digits = 1 hex digit. Remember: '3 for Oct' (Octo has 3 letters) and '4 for Hex' (Hex has 4 letters!)

acronyms.usage: When listing or recalling all four major number systems in order of base value (2,8,10,16)
The leftmost bit in a binary number; carries the highest place value. Determines sign in signed representation.
The rightmost bit in a binary number; carries the lowest place value. Changes most frequently in counting.
Each decimal digit (0-9) is represented by its 4-bit binary equivalent. Used in financial displays.
Standard method for representing signed integers in computers. Range: -(2^(n-1)) to (2^(n-1) - 1) for n bits.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a staircase where each step doubles (for binary) or multiplies by the base. For base-n, each position represents n^0, n^1, n^2... going left from the 'units' step.
Picture a clock face from 0 to 255 (for 8-bit). Going clockwise adds, going counter-clockwise subtracts. -1 = 255, -2 = 254. The wrap-around represents the cyclic nature.
Think of light bulbs toggling. Rightmost bulb changes every count (1,0,1,0...). Each bulb to the left toggles only when the bulb to its right changes from 1→0 (carrying).
Split hex digits into two groups of 4 bits each. Each hex digit directly maps to exactly 4 binary digits — like splitting a 2-digit phone number into area code and local number.

analogies.analogy: Just as English uses 26 symbols (letters) and Chinese uses thousands of characters, computers 'speak' binary using only 2 symbols (0 and 1). Different number bases are just different 'dialects' of the same language.
Think of a bank account with a fixed limit. If you go negative, the number 'wraps around' from 0 to the maximum. 2's complement works similarly — overflow 'debt' wraps around to high positive values.
Like packing 3 items into 1 box. Each octal digit holds exactly 3 binary digits. It's a 'compression format' — fewer characters to write, same information.
An odometer (unsigned) goes from 000 to 999 then rolls over to 000. A signed odometer might go from -500 to +499. Both use the same digits, but the 'zero point' and direction differ.
